caveats: a: The occupation source is four-digit NAICS and includes fruit, vegetable, frozen-food, and other preserving activities.
a: AI-enabled physical automation is included only where it substitutes current labor; conventional capital automation is not treated as AI exposure.
rho: Survey outcomes are self-reported and cross-industry.
rho: Required accountability limits full substitution but still permits substantial decision support and administrative automation.
e: Public directories and company marketing pages demonstrate the model but do not provide an industry denominator.
e: The frozen firm count is an EBITDA-band estimate and may include mixed-industry operations.
s5: Owner intentions are not realized control transfers.
s5: Published food-processing deal counts skew toward larger and disclosed transactions.
q: Contract economics vary materially between retailer private label, foodservice, and emerging-brand customers.
q: No source directly measures five-year retained AI benefit in specialty canning.
d5: Campbell data are company-specific and sales growth is not pure quantity growth.
d5: Birth counts affect only baby food, and pouches or other formats may cross NAICS boundaries.
o: Regulatory accountability does not guarantee that the same independent operator remains the supplier.
o: Customer insourcing is operator displacement and should be measured separately from product-demand decline.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Retailers, foodservice operators, restaurant chains, and consumer-food brands contract for repeated production runs under customer recipes or jointly developed specifications, generally paying per case, unit, batch, or committed volume.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers selling only their own brands, captive production units, brokers and distributors without production responsibility, non-control financing vehicles, and facilities primarily producing fresh, frozen, seafood, meat, or fruit-and-vegetable products classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market specialty-food co-manufacturers and private-label producers providing recurring retort, canning, filling, recipe scale-up, packaging, and quality-assurance services for soups, baked and prepared beans, canned pasta and nationality foods, baby food, and similar shelf-stable products.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment and wages for broader NAICS 311400 rather than task-level AI exposure for 311422 co-manufacturers. The estimate maps exposure to planning, procurement, customer service, finance, recipe and specification documentation, quality records, deviation review, forecasting, and inspection assistance, with lower exposure for physical production, sanitation, maintenance, and material movement. The broader product mix and all-size employer population can bias either direction.
caveats: The occupation source is four-digit NAICS and includes fruit, vegetable, frozen-food, and other preserving activities.
AI-enabled physical automation is included only where it substitutes current labor; conventional capital automation is not treated as AI exposure.
a.change_evidence: Collect payroll-weighted task diaries and system logs from 10-15 independent specialty canners, separating already automated work from remaining tasks in scheduling, QA, R&D, customer service, procurement, warehousing, and line operations.
a.rationale: In broader NAICS 311400, production occupations are 50.95% of employment, transportation/material moving 17.08%, maintenance 9.18%, and office support 5.73%. Specialty co-manufacturing adds customer specifications and formulation workflows suitable for AI assistance, but most direct plant labor remains physical and process-bound.

d5.bridge: Recent company sales and national birth statistics proxy parts of the diverse 311422 basket rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for eligible co-manufacturers. Broth and soup growth supports near-term resilience, while below-replacement fertility constrains baby-food demand; the range allows mix shifts across beans, meals, soups, pasta, foodservice, and private label. Company sales can reflect price and share, so they may overstate quantity growth.
caveats: Campbell data are company-specific and sales growth is not pure quantity growth.
Birth counts affect only baby food, and pouches or other formats may cross NAICS boundaries.
d5.change_evidence: Acquire Circana/Nielsen unit-volume series by shelf-stable soup, broth, beans, canned pasta/meals, and baby food, then combine with deflated shipment volumes and customer forecasts from eligible co-manufacturers.
d5.rationale: Campbell reported broth sales up 15% in a 2025 quarter amid increased at-home cooking, while CDC recorded 3.62 million births in 2024, up 1% year over year but with fertility still below replacement. These mixed indicators support broadly stable five-year basket demand rather than a strong directional claim.

caveats: Public directories and company marketing pages demonstrate the model but do not provide an industry denominator.
The frozen firm count is an EBITDA-band estimate and may include mixed-industry operations.
e.change_evidence: Map all 27 dataset-estimated LMM firms and verify co-manufacturing/private-label revenue through customer contracts, retailer product records, SQF audit profiles, capacity decks, and management interviews.
e.rationale: Specialty canning includes many branded product manufacturers, which fail the outsourced-service lens, but private-label and co-manufacturing are visible operating models across beans, soups, pasta, baby food, and shelf-stable meals. Mixed firms qualify only where recurring external contract production is a material, transferable business activity.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311422 ($861,412,627) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311422 ($7,215,254,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $749,014 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311422); class <5: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $688,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $105,022 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,940,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $448,486 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,560,273 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $847,942 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,440,737 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,269,712 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,656,625 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,182,635 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $416,120,133 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,458,320 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 27

o.bridge: FDA rules demonstrate that the physical processor retains registration, scheduled-process, preventive-control, and oversight duties; they do not measure the year-five quantity share requiring an eligible operator. The estimate translates these physical and accountable obligations into a high retained-operator share. It can overstate retention if customers insource or products migrate to fresh/frozen formats, though such basket loss is primarily handled in d5.
caveats: Regulatory accountability does not guarantee that the same independent operator remains the supplier.
Customer insourcing is operator displacement and should be measured separately from product-demand decline.
o.change_evidence: Interview procurement and operations leaders at retail, restaurant, and CPG customers about insourcing, software substitution, format migration, regulatory responsibility, and five-year co-manufacturer dependence.
o.rationale: Specialty canned foods require recipe scale-up, cooking or retort processing, filling, sealing, testing, sanitation, and accountable release. FDA requires covered canned-food establishments and scheduled processes to be registered and filed, and preventive controls to be overseen. Software can augment this work but cannot independently manufacture and take responsibility for the food.

caveats: Contract economics vary materially between retailer private label, foodservice, and emerging-brand customers.
No source directly measures five-year retained AI benefit in specialty canning.
q.change_evidence: Review customer contracts and five-year renewal histories for price formulas, cost pass-through, productivity clauses, audit rights, terms, minimum volumes, customer concentration, and post-automation margin realization.
q.rationale: Fixed per-case or batch pricing allows temporary retention of labor and error-reduction benefits, while proprietary formulations, responsive R&D, reliability, and food-safety performance can preserve part of the gain. Retail and brand customers can reclaim visible savings through open-book costing, annual negotiations, competitive bids, or renewal repricing, particularly where customer concentration is high.

rho.bridge: Deloitte measures smart-manufacturing results and priorities across manufacturers, not five-year implementation of AI-exposed tasks at LMM specialty canners. Reported productivity outcomes establish feasibility, then the estimate is discounted for legacy equipment, fragmented data, validation needs, food-safety controls, customer approvals, and smaller-firm resources. The survey likely overstates lens implementation because larger manufacturers are better represented and smart manufacturing includes non-AI tools.
caveats: Survey outcomes are self-reported and cross-industry.
Required accountability limits full substitution but still permits substantial decision support and administrative automation.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain five-year deployment and realized-hour data for ERP/MES copilots, machine vision, predictive maintenance, formulation workflows, quality-document generation, and customer-order automation at specialty canners, including abandoned pilots and audit findings.
rho.rationale: Deloitte respondents reported 7%-20% employee-productivity improvement and 10%-15% unlocked capacity from smart manufacturing; 46% ranked process automation among their top two priorities. Implementation remains bounded by product changeovers, recipe confidentiality, exception handling, and legally required food-safety oversight.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among 311422 co-manufacturers. The 22% employer rate anchors the base and is widened for intention failure, unplanned exits, and specialty-food buyer appetite. Intentions likely overstate completions, while off-market and unplanned sales produce some downward offset.
caveats: Owner intentions are not realized control transfers.
Published food-processing deal counts skew toward larger and disclosed transactions.
s5.change_evidence: Construct a denominator-based 2021-2026 ownership cohort for eligible specialty canners using PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, state records, lender data, and food-industry broker interviews, excluding closures, minority investments, and internal reorganizations.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Food-processing deals increased from 20 in 2023 to 52 in 2024, indicating a functioning acquisition market but not a 311422 transfer rate.

principal_driver: Customer-specific formulations and regulated physical production create recurring, operator-required co-manufacturing relationships.
principal_weakness: The eligible universe is small and heterogeneous, while powerful customers can reclaim visible productivity savings.
